CURRENT AFFAIRS.

We have received yet another No. 1, Vol. I.
Knickerbockers are associated with extreme youth, and
the latest “long-felt want’’ to make its bow to the
Anglo-Trish public, though it is called the “ National
Democrat,’’ conjures up before us the vision of a paper
in knickerbockers. Like the late lamented long-felt
want ’’ “ Dana,”’ the new speculation is a monthly pub-
lication; but “Dana’’ was sixpence whilst the latest
“comic’’ is only a penny. Though Democratic,”’
there is evidence of plenty of intellectual snobbery in
it, and though “national’’ we fail to find even one
‘word of Irish in its pages. The bold, bad Dublin Cor-
poration is roundly denounced for its resolution re the
French infidels’ outrage on religion; and Alderman
Cole, “one of the principals’ of Mr. Sweetman’s party,
is clapped on the back for his “thoughtful speech.’”
There is something very cheap, if not decidedly mean,
in clutching hold of a dead man who' cannot protest,
and without leave or licence placing him on your side.
Tt may be common like other cheap tricks. Men will say
__if Parnell, or if Gladstone, or if Cardinal Manning,
ete., etc., were alive, he would see eye to eye with them

» »
”» ”» ”
i 2

”» 2

on some matter or other. Itis handy for ademagogue in
‘want of original argument to lay hold of a dead man’s

A A

guict for ever, works his name for all it is worth. Skeffy
is of opinion that Davitt would have been driven to
found a party and a newspaper ‘to fight the twin
tyrannies which oppress Ireland—the British Govern-
ment and the Catholic Hierarchy.”” Evidently, now
that Michael Davitt is no more, the aspirant for the
chairmanship of the Young Ireland Branch of the
U.ILL. is determined to take on the job himself. We
have already had a taste of Skeffy’s powers as a deadly
enemy to the British Governmen{ in Ireland, when e
looked simply daggers from the gallery at the “loyal
organ of the Royal University, and when he harangued
the atmosphere in a moving-on waggonette. ~ Doubtless
one look of scorn from him would unnerve the Irish
Hierarchy! If he succeeded in creeping under the
table at the next meeting of the Bishops, and popped
up his head all of a sudden, we feel sure their lordships
would be reduced at once into a proper state of moral
pulp quite satisfactory to the New Democracy, and that
one of ““the tyrannies that oppress Ireland ”’ would be
rendered innocuous. Skeffy goes on:—“ With Davitt
gone, such a party is no longer a practicable entity—
merely an aspiration. There is no one left with Davitt’s
unique, stainless record of unselfish service in the cause
of Ireland.””  Of course, that is only the hyphened
democrats’ banter; for no sooner has the reader finished
the sentence than the echo is expected to answer—
‘Skeffy!  This latest journalistic spec., having the
advantage of being dressed in knickerbockers, may suc-
ceed in making the pace for others who have half a
mind for the priest-hunting job. Skeffy’s “ mates’’—
we think that is the  democratic’ term—had better
hurry up if they are not to be altogether out-distanced
in the race. We wish the “National Hierarchy Hunter’’
all the success it deserves.

Mr. John Sweetman has rushed into print by way of
replying to Mr. Stephen Gwynn’s remarks at a recent
U.IL. ward meeting in Dublin. Evidently Mr.
Sweetman is getting nervous of being Vice-President of
an organisation of which Alderman Cole is one of the
loudest official spokesmen.  Mr. Sweetman says—“I
do not think that Mr. Edward Martyn has ever claimed
to be leader of a party.””  Well, then, why has he
allowed himself to be President of the self-styled
National Council? And surely with all due respect
to Mr. Sweetman Mr. Edward Martyn might be allowed
to speak for himself on the matter. Mr. Sweetman
says— I certainly have never claimed to be one of the
leaders of any party.”’ Then why is he Vice-President
of the National Council? We would be sorry to sug-
gest that Messrs. Martyn and Sweetman are merely
pawns placed on the Board by more astute persons,
yet that appears to be the only reading of their case
on Mr. Sweetman’s own showing. So long as Mr.
Martyn’s party had the barking more or less to them-
selves, Mr. Sweetman may have felt happy; now that
others are beginning to bark, and perhaps giving an

382

odd bite in ‘.nIle bargain, Mr. Sweetman announces that
he never claimed to be one of the leaders of any party,
and he states that he does not think that Mr. Edward
Martyn ever claimed to be leader of a party. What,
then, are the President and one of the Vice-Presidents
of this National Council? Are they a pair of children,
a brace of decoy-ducks, two harmless figureheads, use-
ful for heading an appeal for subscriptions, or what?

_ . Mr. John Redmond, as Chairman of the Irish Par-
liamentary Party, is naturally looked upon as the chief
figure in the Irish Parliamentary world, and is ‘usually
referred to as the leader of the Nationalist political
party; of course, anyone is quite entitled
to say that he is a leader only in name, yeb
such suggestions are an adverse reflection upon him,
and are, when made, manifestly intended as such. Bub
here is Mr. Sweetman getting quite ruffled because he
is naturally put up as one of the leaders of an organisa-
tion of which he is Vice-President, and here he is stating
his view that the President, Mr. Martyn never claimed
to be leader. Mr. John Sweetman refers to “one of
the principals >’ of the party, Alderman Cole. Well,
is Alderman Cole the real leader, then, and are Mr.
Sweetman and Mr. Martyn only puppets! Alderman
Cole, “one of the principals”’ of Mr. Martyn’s party,
delivered an harangue in the debate on the vote against
the French Government’s attack on the Church, which
the Zrish Times was not slow to lay hold of for the pur-
pose of attempting to frighten -the Catholic clergy.
Mr. Sweetman, though Vice-President of the party that
the [rish Times says is “ influenced to a striking degree
by the doctrines of continental socialism,”” says he is
not a leader, but that Alderman Cole is “one of the
principals.”  We don’t_think that Mr. Martyn and
Mr. Sweetman can throw off their responsibility so
easily by asserting that they are not leaders, leaving
the public to infer that they are only being used as
decoy-ducks for such gentlemen as “one of the prin-
cipals ** like Alderman Cole. Mr. Martyn is President
and Mr. Sweetman is Vice-President, so we shall con-
tinue to refer to the party of which Alderman Cole is
“one of the principals’’ as Mr. Martyn’s or Mr.
Sweetman’s party, just as in referring to the Irish Par-
liamentarians we may, and probably frequently will,
use the terms, Mr. John Redmond’s party, or Mr. Red-
mond’s and Mr. Dillon’s party.

»

Cashel of the Kings, indeed! Should it not be rather
called in these days Cashel of the Coons? There was a
concert in Cashel City Hall on Thursday evening last,
January 24th. Amongst the 24 items, there was not
one song in Irish. It is a wonder that the Rock of
Cashel didn’t pitch a 100-ton slice of itself on the roof
that covered the West Britons that night. A Mr.
Pullan sang an alleged “ humorous song "’ by the name
of “Two Boys from Cork,”” and another item was
“ Coon costume song and dance,”” by the name of “ O,
‘Mr. Moon.”  Well, Mr. Moon is a bigger fool than we
take him to be if he did not indulge in a sarcastic laugh
at the Rock of Cashel Coons. A Dr. Russell sang
“The Old Grey Fox;’’ now we would not be too hard
on him if he had sung “ Old Alf Fox.”” There was a
Dutch Coster song and dance.” The Dutch are “ tone’”
in Cashel; indeed, we suppose the Zulus would be
honoured there, any people, in fact, but the Irish from

- whom many of the singers, no doubt, have ‘sprung.
‘There was yet another “humorous song’’ by a Dr.
Morgan D’Arcy, called “Paddy’s Weddin’.”” Ts it not
‘very funny that Paddy should get married; perhaps it
is a joke that anyone should have a “ weddin’ ’’ but

_ Messrs: Sawbones. A Mr. Pullan sang “Cake Walk

* Sue.” Might we suggest that at the next concert in

. Cashel someone should sing a “humorous song,’”
‘entitled “ Cashel of the Kings and of the Koons.’’

‘An TIrish reader in England Writes:—“ Will you
~ kindly inform me in your column for correspondents if
 there is a journal (other than the quarterly) in connec-
ion with the Irish Board of Technical Education. If
, where is it published? You are continually callin,

tention to the importation of English and Scotch to

fill vacancies under Board. Where do they obtain in-
formation of theso vacancies? I cannot see it in any
educational journals here.—Thanking you, ete.”  We
do not know of any other journal in_ connection with
the Dopartment. If all these vacancies, as.t’hey. arise
in various parts of the country, were a,clvernvsed in the
Leaper, knowledge of them wou!d be placegl in the way
of a thinking and educated public from w}nch naturally
are drawn the candidates for such vacancies.

Tt is not all well with Humphrey’s Dump, and _the
ouarantors have every reason to feel nervous, particu-
lbzu-}y any of them ‘who may have ;ulready suffered in the
National Assurance Co. It is evidently Dump, Dump,
Dump all the time with Humpl}l‘ey. In dcﬁaqce of the
public voice at the initial meeting at the Antient Con-
cert Rooms, Flumphrey was dumped on the c‘oum.r'y,
Now it would appear that Mr. Rogers and Mr.M‘Guckin
were dumped into the organ loft or some such place, and
Messrs. Culwick, Esposito, Joze, Barl'y' :n}d Seyn_lour,
musicians, have taken counsel with their international
hearts, and have cocked up their tuneful cars, and have
chanted ¢ We Won’t Play any More.”” The Earl of

Drogheda and a Mr, Alfred Manning have kicked over

the traces, and Alf Foz has written a leading article in
which he sighs and lifts his little finger at Mr. Shanks.

The future of this ill-conceived enterprise is very
problematical, and the’ guarantors will only have Lhen}-
selves to blame if they have to pay heavily f_or their
folly. The last paragraph of the letter which M.
Alfred Manning sent to the press has a rather ominous
ring about it. It is as follows: —“ It is, T thm_k, high
time that the Executive Council, as representing t/:‘u
guarantors, should appreciate the seriousness of the
situation which has been created by the two gentlemen
who have caused the present crux, as the responsibility
of failure will ultimately rest upon the shoulders of
that body.”” The italics are ours. Mr. Manning, who
according to his own statement, has given “some three
years’ hard and enthusiastic work in the interests of
the enterprise,”’ ought to know something about the
inner workings and the inevitable bickerings that
accompany a project of this kind, and it must be rather
disquieting to the guarantors to read such phrases as
“the responsibility of failure.”” Is it too late to aban-
don the whole business now? If it is going to be a
failure, what is the use of the guarantors throwing good
money after bad? It is true that a lot of money has
been spent on the structure, but if the guarantors paid
Humphrey to take his dump away in the night, and
with as little noise as possible, might it not be wise on
their part to limit their loss at the present juncture?

When there is a bone to be thrown, expectant dogs
begin to growl at each other. ~The job of organist
was, no doubt, a bone with a fair amount of meat about
it. There is also the catering. The Ivish Times says—
“There is unhappily reason to believe that the treat-
ment complained of by our correspondents is not con-
fined to any one Committee, but that it has been expe-
rienced by several. At the present moment, we'believe,
the appointment of a caterer to the Exhibition—a ques-
tion obviously of great importance to many traders in
Dublin—is causing considerable friction between the
Refreshments Committee and the Executive.” We are
almost moved to exclaim— Poor Humphrey !”’

Mr. William Murphy has plucked up courage to say

“boo ”’ to Arnott’s paper, and has written a reply to
the musicians and others. Mr. Shanks, we are quite

, willing to believe, is pestered by applicants for jobs.

‘M. Murphy says:—“Tt would be easy for Mr. Shanks
o gain popularity at the expense of the enterprise by
“being complaisant to' the many who have their own
axes o grind, but one of the most useful qualities he
possesses for the particular job he has in hands is, in

my judgment, his sturdy and fearless character.”’. All
[traces of the Murphy have not been knocked out of the

Chairman of the Tram Co. as a consequence of close

“application to dividend-making and directors’ fee-earn-

1ng, for one can see the Irish passion of a moment flush-
ing the cheeks of ‘this director, of the Gureat Sourface,
Rz:,llway‘ when he tells Arnott’s paper:—* T belieyo Mr.
M‘Guckin to. be eminently suited for, the office to which
he has been. appointed, although he, has an, obtrusively
;rlsh name; and if the appointment is a means of help-
ing him to make a living in his native city, it should
be no cause. of objection.”” That is one between the
eyes for Sir John Arnott and Mz, Algernon Locker,
anyway.

Four of the protesting musicians—Esposito, Joze,
Seymour, and Culwick—may feel inclined to boycott,
the trams after that, for wherever they may have been
born they cannot be said to have * obtrusively Irish’’
names !

Mr. Murphy raps Arnott’s paper over the knuckles
for its reference in its leading article to Lady Aberdeen.
And concerning the catering job, Mr. Murphy says:—
“You are 3 misinformed when you state that the
catering contract is causing considerable friction between
the Refreshments Committee and the Executive. This
business is being considered in consultation with the
Committees, and, like every other business, will be
dealt with in the best interests of the Exhibition enter-
prise alone, and without regard to any private interest.’

Mr. Murphy administers what, in juvenile circles, is,
we believe, called “a hiding ”’ to Mr. Alfred Manning,
Grafton Street, for he writes that there is “a numeri-
cally insignificant minority, of whom Mxy. Alfred Man-
ning is one, who have lately been endeavouring to give
trouble without success.””  The least Mr. Manning
could do by way of retort is to send a stuffed tiger to
Mr. Murphy, for Mr. Manning is in the wild beast
fur importing lines. Alas, poor Dump!

The recent municipal elections in Athlone appear to
have been quite tame affairs; but then our contem-
porary, the “ Westmeath Independent,’’ tells us that—
“ The police took the precaution of locking up a couple
of the characters that might create disturbance, and
this helped to maintain the good order which prevailed
throughout the day. It was evident that the bowl did
not flow as freely as it used to on those annual occasions.
There was hardly a sign of drink about the place, and
the election was a ‘dry’ one, as one old thirst described
it.””  Poor Bung is getting knocked on every side these
hard times. Our contempoarry tells us further that
when the results of the election were announced— “There
was some cheering indulged in, but it came principally
from the juvenile element which, predominated in. the
crowd.”” What are municipal politics coming to? If

. all boys between the ages of eight and twelve had votes

the prevailing apathy might lift somewhat.

We take the following from an English paper:—
“ Newcastle-upon-Tyne Watch Committee yesterday
decided to prohibit police officers receiving testimonials
either while in or leaving the force.””  There are some
things, anyway, that they do in England that might,
with advantage, be done over here also.

The position of lecturer in Irish at the Queen’s Col-
lege, Cork, was held for a number of years by the dis-
tinguished Irish scholar, Dr. Oshorne Bergin. Dr.
Bergin recently accepted the, position of Director of the

THE LEABER.

383

School of Irish Learning in Dublin, and the position, he,
vacated at the. Queen’s College, Cork, has now been,
filled by the appointment, to it of Mr. €amon O "Oonns
cdoa, The new lecturer in Irish is well known in Irish:
language circles for his solid attainmemts. We are.
puzzled whether to say that €amon is a brother to
*Topna,” or that ““ Topna ”* is.a brother to €smon.

The President of Maynooth, Monsignor Mannix, in
the course of his address at the Prevention of Intem-
perance Meeting at the Rotunda, remarked that a short
timo ago the public-houses, “ which had ruined, to a.
large extent, the cities and the towns, was following
the people into the country districts, and bringing the.
liquor to the very doors of the rural people.”” He went
on:—

It scemed a3 if the logislators wero overawed by the power of
the liquor interest when they tried to take measures against it;
and ovon religion itsell scemed to bo unable to cope with the
power of the drink evil. It was a sad spectacle—a nation with
her history and aspirations, susceptible to the highest religious
feeling, scemed to be unable to shake olf the paralys’s and slavery
of drink. Thero was cffort-—-hore and there in the country were
clergymen, active workers, keeping temperanco socicties, and
doing all that in them lay to direct attention to the necessity for
reform—but there was no energy adequato to deal with the evil—
drink flowed, and the country scemed to be in a stupor and blind
to its degradation.  But, notwithstanding what thoy sometimes
heard, he was convinced that they had lived to sce the dawn of a
happier day (loud applause)—a day in which legislators and
voters, laymen and clerics, were more interested in the temper-
anco question than ever sinco the break-up of the Father Mathew
movement,

Myr. William Redmond, who, despite the Bungs of
Ennis, has played a manly part in the Temperance Cam-
paign,. proposed—* That this meeting heartily approves
of the policy of the Irish Association for the Preven-
tion of Intemperance.””  And he remarked that the
resolution was acceptable to fully one-half of the mem
bers of the Irish Parliamentary Party. Only one-half!
Mr. Redmond in stating that touchéd on one of the
weakest spots of the Irish Parliamentary Party. The
Irish Party in the Imperial Parliament is too largely
a pro-Bung party. No doubt, they get plenty of finan-
cial support from Bung, for Buug is an off-hand,
generous sort of fellow, and probably if they went
strong against Bung, the Temperance Party would be
largely content to let virtue be its own reward. Very
little thanks, not to talk of more material recompenses
we get, or ever got, for bearding Bung in his den, and
going out to take one of the most powerful forces in
TIreland on our shield. However, be that as it_may, the
Inish Party, if it wishes to be regarded as a national
and patriotic party, should show a bigger percentage
than—if we accept Mr. W. Redmond’s figures as exact
—fifty per cent. against one of the main forces that is
keeping Ireland in the mire. Such men as Mr. J. J.
Clancy, Mr. Tim Total Harrington, Mr. Pat O’Brien,
and Meagher of the Pint are Bung’s most obedient ser-
vants in the ranks of the Irish Parliamentary Party.

The drinking clubs are a great curse to Ireland, and
have brought untold misery in many a home. Refer-
ring to these clubs, Mr. T. W. Russell, the Scotchman,
said :—“ As to the clubs, they were in nine cases out of
ten a fraud. Why were they not subject to laws and
rules and hows? e would tell them. Because the
Kildare Street Club would have to be subject to them.

And so in order to protect the Kildare Street Club and
Stephen’s Green Club all this devilry must go on
throughout the city. This club nuisance must |

ended.”” The bungery attached to grocers and other
shops is another intolerable evil that calls for attention.

It is quite common now for temperance orators to pay
a tribute to the influence of the Gaclic League; ib is
S0 common, indeed, that it is almost becoming mono-
tonous. Yet with all this lip-homage, we find that on
the musical programme at the meeting of the Irish
Association for prevention of Intemperance, which was
held at Mr. Carthy’sshop in Grafton Street, there was
not as much as one song in Irish. It is all very well
to talk about the influence of the Gaelic League, but the
least that might have been expected of some of those
present at the function was that they should have seen
to it that the Irish language was not boycotted.

For one reason or another this all-creeds-all-lasses
Association of which Mr, Moriarty, the bankrupt, was
secretary, irritates our “temperament.”’. In the offi-
cial report the Catholics are referred to as “ Roman
Catholics,” and the following paragraph does not help
to soothe us:—“ On Wednesday, the 16th May, 1906,
the deputation was received at the Viceregal Lodge,
Dublin, by his Excellency the Earl of Aberdeen, who
was accompanied by Lady Aberdeen, and at-
tended by Lord Herschell, Private Secretary;
Mr. Bowen Colthurst, Assistant Private Secretary ;
Captain the Hon. A. H. Ruthven and Lord Anson,
A.D.C.’s-in-Waiting.””  There is a great deal of the
spirit of the West British Flunkey about that para-
graph. It is a wonder the report did not set out that
Lord Herschell looked charming in silk stockings, that
the silver buckles of the Hon. A. H. Ruthven were +
dream, and that Mr. Bowen Colthurst looked simply
lovely through an eye-glass and a lot more of that sort
of stuff which is dearly loved by the bon ton of Kenil-
worth Park.

We were promised some time ago an early publication
of the accounts of the All-Ireland Bazaar. However,
the accounts have not been published, as far as we
know. We read the following paragraph in the report—
“The accounts of the All-Treland Temperance Bazaar,
when finally audited, showed that the gross profits
amounted to over £11,000, the expenditure to over
£5,000; leaving a net profit of £6,178 4s. 4d.  This
sum has been equally divided between our Association
and the Irish Temperance League. We have each voted
£250 for the development especially of Temperance
catering through the country as approved by the Irish
National Temperance Executive, who have made 28
grants, amounting in all to £425.” This is scarcely
good enough. What about the details?

The Workmen’s Club, which occupied premises at 15
Upper Gloucester Street, has gone smash; and the
Shamrock Dining Rooms have failed. The guarantors
are being applied to in the latter case. It was found
impossible to dispose of the property, so the late manager
of them, Miss Louise Pinkerton, has taken them over
at a rental. We read :— The Association were obliged
to devote, in addition to the £400 invested last year,
£589 15s. in paying the debts due by the dining rooms.
This has been a keen disappointment, especially as it
was hoped they would pay a fair interest on their deben-
tures, of which there seems little prospect.”” Certainly
we agree it is a keen disappointment. But we would
like to hear some explanation as to the cause of this
collapse.

The Report of the Belfast Association contains some
useful statistics. Amongst Irish cities Limerick has the
unwelcome honour of being the most drunken. The
number of drunks in Limerick amounted to 1,586;
Derry comes next with 1,656, which is lower per cent.
of the population than Limerick; Waterford is a good
third, its drunks numbering 1,006; Cork comes next,

‘then Belfast, and the soberest of the lot, it is hard for a

resident to believe, is Dublin. Within the Dublin
Metropolitan Police district, representing a population
of about 393,000 'the number of drunks was 4,606,
whilst in Belfast, 349,000, the number of drunks totalled
4,785, so that the town of the ford and hurdles has a
c;nsidemble advantage over the town of' t}k’le nuts and
bolts, where “the civic virtues and. c_ﬂicwncles are a_lleged
to come from. As for the counties, ‘Waterford is the
most drunken county in Ireland, Kildare comes next,
then Queen’s County, Limerick, ]?crl'y, Kilkenny, Tip-
perary South, King’s County, Claro, Tyrone, West-
meath, Monaghan, Tipperary, North Donegal, Keljry,
Cavan, Armagh, Longford, Co'rk, Fermanagh, Leitrim,
Galway, Wicklow, Doyn, Dublin, Meath, Sl{go, Carlow,
Roscommon, Wexford, Antrim, Louth, Cork, (West
Riding), Mayo. These results do not suggest any com-
ment from tho Irish language point of view, for Watelr.
ford tops tho list, Mayo is at the bottom, and Kerry is
about mid-way.

The syllabus of the peir Llo¢ Capmdmn for 1907
is already published. The Feip will be held in Wex-

We would like to call the attention of our readers to
the public meeting to be held at the Mansion House on
to-morrow (Friday) night in connection with the Catho-
lic Boys’ Home. The Lord Mayor will preside, and
his Grace the Archbishop has promised to attend. The
Catholic Boys’ Home in Abbey Street provides cheap
meals and beds for boys. There are practically no re-
strictions in the dispensing of meals, which run in price
from 1d. to 3d.; of course, in the matter of beds—the
charge is, we believe, one penny a night—there are rules
and regulations. The fact that last year’s revenue from
the boys reached about £250—mostly made up of
pennies, we presume—indicates how largely the benefits
of this Boys’ Home are availed of.

The Rathmines and Rathgar Catholic Association are
now installed in their new premises, 81 Rathmines
Road. We are glad to learn that the Association is in
a flourishing condition, and that its prospects are very
promising. The Association’s Annual Reunion will be
held on next Tuesday in the. rooms of the D.B.C.,
O’Connell Street. Like that of last year, it will take
the form of a dance; and we are sure that many who
attended last season’s dance will avail themselves of the
opportunity of another pleasant evening, which the com-
ing one will afford. We would like to point out that
the Reunion will not be confined to members of the Asso-
ciation. Well-wishers who are not members can obtain
tickets (7s. 6d. double; 5s. single) on application to
the hon. secretary, 81 Rathmines Road, or from
members of the Association.

Passions are inflamed now, and the Abbey Theatre
people, we see, have decided to fight the maftter oub.
The manifesto they have issued may indicate pluck,
but we do not think it is wise. They made a mistake
in staging the play, and having staged it the reception
which its first performance got from the audience, and
from the Press, should have convinced the authorities
of the theatre of the wisdom of withdrawing the piece.

The issue is now very much confused, and the pas-
sions that have been aroused only makes far more con-
fusion. Many of those who object strongly to vhe play
as a slander on Irish life, and a thing unfit for t'e
stage, may not endorse the methods adopted on Monday
night, and yet, their feelings of revulsion being strong,
they cannot see their way to unreservedly condemn such
tactics. An organised effort to stop a performance in
a theatre or anywhere else, has to be taken on its
merits; if the general feeling is strong such opposition
will meet with a considerable amount of sympathy, and
is different altogether from an organised clique who,
in defiance of general opinion, would attempt to prevent
a performance held in a hall from which the public
were free to stay away.

The authorities of the theatre endeavour to elevate
the squabble into one of Organised Opposition versus
Freedom of Judgment. Might we point out that though
some of the opposition may have been organised, the
spontaneous and independent opposition is very great
and very strong, and that “freedom of judgment
sounds all right as a phrase, but will not necessarily
convince people who regard it as a misnomer for fi
dom of slander, and for freedom for hurling obs:
insults at the peasants of Mayo; indeed, as the pea-
santry of Ireland are a class in themselves—and the
biggest class in Ireland—the freedom of judgment’’
indulged in by Mr. Synge is a matter that concerns all
the mere Irish. Mr. Synge has apparently outraged
Irish feeling, and the authoritics of the theatre are, we
think, ill-advised in fighting the cause of what they call
“freedom of judgment’’ with such a weapon as “The
Playboy of the Western World.”

POOR TEINITY!

W OOR Beatrice!’’ is the heading Thackeray gives

the chapter wherein he tells us how that domi-
neering and unscrupulous woman ended by losing
cverything, and we may feel a like pity for the idle,
selfish daughter of Dublin University in her sore dis-
tress. Where now are her Traills and her Aftkinsons,
her Catholics and her Kellehers? Modo domina gentium.
Poor Trinity! That it should come to this. Trinity
reduced to be a college of the Royal University! For,
when you take away the trimmings that is what it all
comes to. A reformed, disinfected, rechristened
Royal University, but the Royal University all
the same. The name is gone, but the thing remains;
and though little enamoured of the present scheme, few
things have given me'keener pleasure than the spreading
pallour of my Trinity acquaintances as I made clear
this fact to them. Ilwwm futurum fuerit. Whakt is
the Royal University? I have often explained. It is
not the examinations; it is not Earlsfort Terrace. It
is in essence the federation of four very dissimilar col-
leges in an irksome combination. The Robertson Com-
mission proposed to alleviate the evil to a greater or
lesser degree by making the colleges more or less auto-
nomous. The new Government scheme (apart from
details) adopts the Robertson plan with two important
modifications. It adds Trinity (poor Trinity!) as a fifth
college ; it calls the reformed University Dublin Univer-
sity, which it evidently is not, instead of the Royal
University, which it most undoubtedly is—wvoila tout.
You may talk for ever, but that is what it really means.
It has, indeed, suggested that the alleged Catholic col-
lege is to be a mere hostel with some tutors, a trimming
to Trinity, but as I read it nothing so entirely prepos-
terous and intolerable is contemplated.

From an cducational point of view the scheme
is, indeed, ‘as it is, palpably absurd, more
absurd even than the Robertson scheme. A
University  scattered  between  three cities is
a monster of the worst type. One could only hope
that, like other fabulous monsters, it would have the
faculby of splitting up into its component parts. The
educational needs of Ireland most clearly ‘demand four
Universities of the non-collegiate city type, found all
over the world—two in Dublin, one in Cork, one in
Belfast. Something could even be said for a two-col-
lege scheme, though colleges in Universities are an
English exotic.  For this would still be more or less
Dublin University in fact instead of in name. Butb
on educational grounds there is nothing to be said for
the new federal University.  Educational grounds,
however, are, of coutse, the last thing that count iw
the government of Ireland, aid on political grounds we
are forced back upon one or other of the two vicious
federal schemes. That is the reason why, ihstead of
a sensible arrangement of four Universities, such as exist

in Scotland or Switzerland, we are thrown back upon
the present unsatisfactory and uneducational grouping

of four or five colleges.

But the political obstacle
seems impassable. 3 g 3

The Shanachic is a shy publication. It peeps out
only twice a year! That is its greatest {nerib ; 1t can
double its virtues at any time by appearing only once
a year, whilo it can exalt them indefinitely by nob
appearing at all. Tt is not half as good a production
as A Celtic Christmas, for the latter, with wise modesty,

* appears but once a twelvemonth, and is, besides, only

one-fourth of the price of Zhe Shanachie—a literary
effort which would be dear at any price, and is quite

~ too dear at the soaring sum of a shilling. A: shilling?

You could get a cloth-bound volume of any of the
master-pieces of literature for that.
Shanachie contains things that “never appeared
ore.”” Good reason, too, for who would think of

Oh, “but The

THE LEADER. 889" -

printing them twice? That is where the stuff in Z'%e
Shanachie differs from literature; it never appeared
before, and never will again. For the most part, it
may be said that the contributions to Z'he Shamaclie are
so many shots fired at the dun target of Oblivion, and
that all but all of them have hit the centre with rare
precision. Perhaps the only good thing in the volume
is a nice drawing of the Four Courts, Dublin, by Mr.
Hugh Thomson. Indeed, it is good enough to make
one wonder what on earth Mr. Thomson is doing in
that galley.

For a production with half a year for its preparation,
there was probably never anything so poor as this hap-
less Shanachie. It is for all the world like the maga-
zine which the comical clique of literary failures in
Daudet’s Jack brought out. I have said that its con-
tents “ never appeared before,”” and so much is literally
true; not morally, however, since the turgid lecture on
Art, by “A. E.,”” though it did not “ appear ’’ before,
yet was heard before. Is T'he Shanachie going to be a
sarcophagus for old lectures? If so, it need never be
“ gravelled for lack of matter,” since there is always
abundance of that kind of thing to be had for the ask-
ing. Indeed, it were perilous for any editor to invite
such contributions; he would want to hoist an umbrella
to ward them off as he walked the streets. “A.E.’s””
lecture opens in the usual way of the airy literary man
of the Yeats school. Mr. Hugh Lane asked him for a
lecture ; insisted on a lecture; would have a lecture.
What was he to lecture about?  Anything he liked.
And so he let himself be put down for “ Art and Litera-
ture.”” He really didn’t know what he was going to
say, or how he was going to say it, but if his audience
were good-natured, and didn’t expect too much, and—
and—and—so on. Something like that it began; the
heavier metal came afterwards—some of it very heavy,
indeed. If some of that audience didn’t go away with
wsthetic headaches, I'm no true man. Now and then
an acceptable thing is said, but for the greater part the
lecture is like what Hamlet was reading—words, words,
words. - Mr. Russell persistently uses the terms
“ethics ”’ and “morals,”” and “ethical ’’ and “ moral,””
as if they were convertible; which they are not. But
it were a committing of the sin of dulness to say any-
thing more of this matter.

Amongst the contributors to T'he Shanachie is a Miss
Langbridge, daughter of the arranger, or adapter, of
Dickens’s 7'ale of Two Cildes, who christened his adap-
tation The Only Way. The lady presents a squalid tale
in a manner which recalls stage conventions of the
emphatic order, and which breathes the spirit of imita-
tion pantingly hard. She has evidently studied some
of the most “trenchant’’ writers of the short story,
and has acquired quite a slap-bang manner of polishing
off what she apparently conceives to be Irish low life.
It were a sin against the sense of proportion to devote
any more space to this inspired imitatress.

Another contributor is Lord Dunsany. HHis contri-
bution reminds me of Mr. William O’Brien’s policy by
reason of this little peculiarity : that I can’t understand
it. It is a very mystical, strange performance, whereof
the most engaging merit is its shortness. It is called
“The Whirlpool,” and it winds up with the state-.
mont (luminous with italics) that “the gods have not
content.”” Therein do the gods differ from his Lord-
ship, and from the editor of Zhe Shanachie, for the one
was content to write, and the other to accept, this
weird production. Lord D. is a nephew of Sir Horace
Plunkett, and without any intentional asperity I must
say that I prefer the writings of the uncle. As I don’t
understand his Lordship’s drift, it were an offence
against the laws of right reason to say more of the
matter. 3 'k

A masber is the amount of the educational grant
his school is to receive each year. Professors, pupils,
‘and their parents have their curiosity satisfied, as a
rule, early in Soptember when the results pamphlets
- disclose the secrets of the examinations. The head-

But the cry of “cram’’ was raised. This cry was
taken up very largely by those who knew nothing about
the work of the schools. It made an impression on the
Viceregal Commission, and the system of payment by
results fees on individual subjects was condemned. Six
new members wero added to the Board. Many of them
had fads; none of them had experience of conducting a
Secondary School.  The cry of “co-ordination” was
responsible for the appointment of Mr. Starkie to
occupy a Catholic place. This is not the only mischief
caused to the Intermediate Schools by that cry. Pay-
ment by results was abolished ; School Grant was sub-
stituted. The method of ‘determining the School Grant
revealed the astonishing genius for decimals possessed
probably by one of the new members. To avoid caus-
ing hardship to a school by fluctuation of tho grant,
the average of three years was to be taken. This rule
of three years’ average was never acted on, and the
magnificent decimal system, after causing considerable
merriment during its year’s trial, gave way to a system
of payment by results infinitely worse than the old dis-
carded results system. Under the new system the
higher grade schools have been groaning and the lower
grade schools flourishing ever since.

Fair Play would require that the amount of money
paid to any Intermediate School for the year’s work
should be in direct proportion to the amount of Inter-
mediate work done. This proportion was secured by
the old results system. The sum paid for a pass in a
particular subject depended on the gross total of marks
assigned to that subject. Twice as much was paid for
a subject in Middle Grade, and three times as much in
Senior Grade, as for that subject in Junior Grade. As
vhe gross totals varied from 1,200 allotted to Greek,
Latin and English down to 200 allotted to Bock-keep-
ing, there were abundant opportunities of graduating
the payments “ in proportion to the difficulty and educa-
tional value of the subjects dealt with.”” Moreover,
the more subjects a pupil passed in (subject to a fixed
maximum), the more money the school earned, and the
addition of 50 per cent. for each honowr was an induce
ment to the school to bring the pupils up to the honours
standard in as many subjects as possible.

But the cry of “cram’ changed all that. To pre-
vent ““ over-pressure ”’ the amount of the School Grant
was made to depend on the number of passes and passes
with honours in grade. The values assigned to each
pass in grade, and to each pass with honours, were
practically the maximum obtainable under the old
system. To get the maximum pass results in the old
system, a pupil should pass in the following subjects (or
their equivalents) : —Greck, Latih, English (including
Grammar, Composition, Literature, Geography and
History), Arithmetic, Algebra and Geometry (and in
Senior Grade Trigonometry), together with a modern
language, and in the lower grades one additional sub-
jeet. To get the maximum honours results a pupil
should pass with honours in all his subjects. Very few
got honours in all their subjects; a great many did not
cven secure the maximum pass vesults. Schools that
were not able or did not want to undertake the larger
cwriculum received payment proportionate to their
work. On the other hand, schools that found it neces-
sary to teach the more difficult subjects to their pupils
gob a larger reward, and this reward increased with the
number of honours scored in the various subjects What-
ever faults may be found with the old system, it cannot
be denied, abt all events, that it secured an equitable
distribution of the Intermediate funds.

L have stated already that after 1901 full pass
results were obtainable, even though both the classi-
cal subjects and a couple of the mathematical subjects
be dropped from the programme; and that full honours
results were obtainable if a student got honours in three
subjects. Of course, that was too good to last.  The
lowering of the conditions for a pass and the enhanced
value given to it brought new pupils, and even new
schools, into the system. The number of passes in-
creased, and as the fund was limited the fees payable
for a pass were diminishing year by year. The great
crash came in 1905. The scale of 1904 was 6 per cent.
lower than that of 1903 ; the scale of 1905 dfopped 30
per cent. below that of 1904. Thus, by one blow,
schools were brought to the verge of bankruptcy. In
some cases teachers had to appeal to their charitable
friends for payment of their insurance premiums. The
cause of the disaster was twofold. —The number of
passes increased, and the receipts of the Board, from
what is known as the whiskey-money, were reduced.
The head-masters appealed in vain to the Board to
draw upon its reserve of unexpended income to make
good the year’s deficit. Equally vain was their appeal
to Mr. Bryce to save them from fluctuation and to
relieve them from the degradation of dependence on the
consumption ‘of whiskey and beer in the country. I
wonder will the late Minister of Bducation give his
adhesion to the doctrine that the growth of temperance
ought to bring about automatically the starvation of
secondary education.

The Board cannot be blamed for the reduction in the
consumption of spirituous liquor; but there is a griev-
ance against them if, by allowing pupils to pass too
easily, they distribute Intermediate money for primary
work; or if they distribute the money inequitably
among Intermediate Schools. This year, thanks to the
literature guillotine, the number of passes was kept
down practically to last year’s standard, though 772
additional boys were examined. Theguillotine served one
useful purpose, anyhow. Thesame cannot be said of the
tampering with the time-honoured scale of payment. The
fées payable for a pass in Junior, Middle and Senior
Gradés used to be as 1, 2 and 3; this year they ‘are 'as
1, 1} and 2}. The Middle and Senior Grades have
been reduced 25 per cent, as ‘compared 'with Junior.
The reductions, of course, affect honours as well as
passes. Taking into account the smaller number of
students in Middle, and especially in Senior, Grade, the
greater importance and difficulty of the instruction im-
parted, and the greater difficulty and expense of pro-
viding that instruction, I contend that the scale was
not over‘generous in favour of the higher grades. To
the reduction of the Preparatory Grade from two-
thirds to half the Junior pass, I do not object, secing
that the Preparatory Grade pass represents ‘work that
is, or may,be, inferior to what is prescribed for the
Primary Schools.

The result of all the clianges is etitirely in favour of
schools doing Junior Grade work only.  The Junior
Gradoe results fees are. actually greater than those of
1905; in all the other grades the fees have been re-
duced. I have shown that a good deal of Junior Grade
education is really primary education. Hence the
injustice of increasing the reward for that work at the
oxpense of Intermediate work proper is obvious.  If
the scale was to be altered at ‘all it should have been
altered in favour of Semior Grade rather than against

OST people express great surprise at the renewal
of 1l?.hep:l,pp]ili':a,i;iong made by Messrs. Boamish
and Crawford, Cork, for ‘a drink licence or certificate
for their Workingmen’s Club. Indeed, I do not know
of anything that has been more universally condemned
the citizens at large than the effort to obbam' this
licence—people of all classes and creeds feel convinced

CRAWFORD'S

THE LEADER.

FEERUARY 2, 1907.

that, if granted, it would prove most detrimental to
the workers and their families; while none that I have
met are more emphatic in their condemnation of it
than the wives, mothers, and other. members of the
brewerymen’s familics. These have been thanking God
from their hearts that the application made a few
months ago was not granted. ~ Many a one of them
have I heard say— Thank God, they did not get that
licence. It is we would be tho sufferers. Surely their
wages are little enough to keep the home, to support
their wives and families—they have somethi.ng else to
do with their carnings besides spending them in a drink-
ing club.”” From the views thus quite spontaneously
expressed to me on all sides recently, I am perfectly
satisfied that not a single brewery worker’s family
approves of this drinking bar; and, as for the men
themselves, any of them who have spoken to me on the
subject have sensibly remarked—*The club is going
on all right without a drinking licence; why not let
very well alone?’ I find it hard to believe that
Messrs. Beamish and Crawford will persist in their
application if they are aware of the feeling of those
who are chiefly concerned and who understand perfectly
well that this licence would prove disastrous to many
families and homes—nor do I believe that our city
magistrates will be so blind to the interests of the
workers as to place this terrible temptation in the way
of men who are already more strongly tempted to drink
than any other class in the community. Who can
know the evils of drink better than the city magistrates?
Day after day they are engaged in the monotonous and
sickening duty of fining and imprisoning men and
women who are degraded by drink. Is it credible that
at the beck of any brewer they will set up another temp-
tation of the very worst and most dangerous kind, and
which, humanly speaking, must result in adding to the
very degradation and crime which come under their
notice almost daily in the police court? The magistrates,
too, must be aware of the excellent results of the recent
law which has cut off the very worst drinking hours on
Sunday and Saturday nights. ~ The organs of the Press,
as well as the police authorities, are unanimous in
declaring these results to be most satisfactory. My
own personal experience leaves no room for doubt on
the subject. ~Will the magistrates of Cork commit
themselves to a step which, of all others, is most cal-
culated to destroy these happy results, or rather, will
they not say to themselves as Irishmen, and as men
invested by the law with authority and power to serve the
people—“God forbid that we, by any act of ours,
should do anything else than aid our fellow-countrymen
in their efforts to deliver themselves from this wretched
drink thrall ¥’
P. O’Leary, C.C.
